phase_metadata.title:
Inheritance and the Object
Class
phase_metadata.description:
Master the art of creating class hierarchies, reusing
code through the extends keyword, and understanding the root of all Java
classes.

learning_objectives:
Understand
the 'IS-A' relationship in Object-Oriented Programming

Implement single
inheritance using the extends keyword

Utilize the super keyword to access
parent members and constructors

Perform Method Overriding to redefine
behavior in subclasses

Apply the final keyword to prevent inheritance or
overriding
Use the instanceof operator for type checking

Override methods
from the java.lang.Object class like toString() and equals()
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of Classes and Objects (Phase 5)
Understanding of methods and return types

Familiarity with access modifiers
(briefly covered in basics)

introduction.definition:
Inheritance is a mechanism in Java where one class (the subclass
or child) acquires the properties and behaviors (fields and methods) of another
class (the superclass or parent).
introduction.why_we_need_it:
It promotes code
reusability and establishes a natural hierarchy. Instead of writing the same
code for 'Car' and 'Truck', you put common code in a 'Vehicle' class and let
both inherit from it.
introduction.is_a_relationship:
Inheritance represents the IS-A
relationship. For example, a Dog IS-A Animal, a Manager IS-A Employee.

concept_explanation.extends_keyword:
The 'extends' keyword is used to
create a subclass. In Java, a class can extend only one other class (Single
Inheritance).
concept_explanation.super_keyword:
The 'super' keyword refers to the immediate
parent class object. It is used to call parent constructors, parent methods, or
access parent fields that might be hidden by the subclass.
concept_explanation.method_overriding:
Overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific
implementation for a method already defined in its superclass. The method
signature must be identical.
concept_explanation.final_keyword:
When applied to a class,
'final' prevents it from being extended. When applied to a method, it prevents
it from being overridden.
concept_explanation.object_class:
Every class in Java implicitly
extends the 'java.lang.Object' class. It is the root of the entire class
hierarchy.
concept_explanation.instanceof_operator:
A comparison operator used to test whether
an object is an instance of a specific class or subclass.

syntax_breakdown.extends:
Indicates
that the class is deriving from a base class.
syntax_breakdown.super:
A reference variable
used to point to the parent class object.
syntax_breakdown.@Override:
An annotation that
tells the compiler the method is intended to override a parent method. It helps
catch errors if the signature doesn't match.
syntax_breakdown.final:
A modifier that makes a
component unchangeable.

rules_and_key_points:
Java does not support
multiple inheritance with classes (one child, one parent).

Private members of
the superclass are NOT inherited directly by the subclass.

Constructors are
not inherited, but the parent constructor is called during subclass
instantiation.

The call to super() must be the first statement in a subclass
constructor.

A subclass can add its own new fields and methods in addition to
inherited ones.

Overriding methods must have the same name, return type, and
parameters.

An overriding method cannot have a more restrictive access
modifier (e.g., cannot change public to private).

Static methods cannot be
overridden; they are 'hidden' if redefined.

The final keyword on a class
prevents it from being a parent.

The Object class provides methods like
equals(), hashCode(), and toString() to all Java objects.

code_example_1.title: Basic Inheritance (The IS-A Relationship)
code_example_1.description:
A simple
example showing how a Dog class inherits from an Animal class.

code:
This animal eats food.
The dog barks.

code_example_2.title:
Method
Overriding and the 'super' Keyword
code_example_2.description:
A practical example showing
a payroll-like structure where a Manager gets a different bonus calculation.

code.step_3:
Employee constructor initializes 'name' and 'salary' fields in the
object memory.
code.step_4:
Control returns to Manager constructor, which
initializes 'teamSize'.
code.step_5:
mgr.displayDetails() is called. Since
Manager overrides it, the Manager version runs.
code.step_6:
Inside
Manager.displayDetails(), super.displayDetails() calls the Employee version to
print name/salary.
code.step_7:
Manager's displayDetails continues to print the
teamSize.

memory_visualization.heap:
A single object of type 'Manager' is created. Inside
this object space, memory is allocated for inherited fields (name, salary) and
specific fields (teamSize). Even though it's one object, it contains the full
'state' of the parent.
memory_visualization.method_area:
Contains the class metadata for
Employee and Manager, including pointers to the overridden method table
(vtable).

real_world_use_cases:
E-commerce: 'Product' is a superclass;
'Electronics' and 'Clothing' are subclasses with specific attributes.

UI
Frameworks: A generic 'Component' class has draw() and click() methods, while
'Button' or 'Slider' provides specific implementations.

Game Development: A
'Character' superclass handles movement; 'Player' and 'Enemy' inherit movement
but define different attack logic.
common_mistakes:
Trying to extend
multiple classes: Java only allows one parent class.

Forgetting to call
super() in a subclass constructor when the parent doesn't have a default
(no-arg) constructor.

Thinking private methods can be overridden: They
cannot, as they aren't visible to the subclass.

Changing the method signature
(parameters) while intending to override: This creates a 'Method Overload'
instead of an 'Override'.

Using '==' to compare object contents instead of
overriding the equals() method from the Object class.
best_practices:
Always use the @Override annotation when overriding to ensure compile-time
checking.

Follow the 'Liskov Substitution Principle': A subclass should be
able to stand in for a superclass without breaking the program.

Use 'final'
for methods that are critical to security or logic and should never be changed
by a subclass.

Keep the inheritance hierarchy shallow (don't go 10 levels
deep) to maintain readability.

Override toString() in your classes to provide
a meaningful string representation of objects.

frequently_asked_questions.question: Can a subclass access private fields of a superclass?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
No. Subclasses only inherit public and protected members. To access
private fields, the subclass must use public getter and setter methods provided
by the parent.

frequently_asked_questions.question:
What is the difference between Overloading and
Overriding?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
Overloading happens in the same class (same name,
different parameters). Overriding happens in a subclass (same name, same
parameters, same return type).

frequently_asked_questions.question:
What happens if I don't use
super() in my constructor?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
The Java compiler automatically inserts
a call to the parent's no-argument constructor: super();. If the parent doesn't
have a no-argument constructor, you will get a compile-time error.

code_example_1.summary:
Inheritance is the backbone of Java's reusability, allowing classes
to share code through hierarchies. By using 'extends', 'super', and '@Override',
developers can build complex systems that are easy to maintain and extend
without duplicating logic.
key_takeaways: Java supports single inheritance only for classes.

The 'super'
keyword accesses parent constructors and members.

Method overriding allows
specialized behavior in subclasses.

The @Override annotation prevents
signature mismatch errors.

Final classes cannot be inherited; final methods
cannot be overridden.

The 'instanceof' operator checks an object's type at
runtime.
All classes inherit from the Object class by default.
Constructors are not inherited but are called in sequence (parent first).
code_example_1.preview_of_next_topic:
In Phase 7, we will explore Polymorphism, which allows us to treat objects of
different subclasses as objects of a common superclass, enabling dynamic and
flexible code execution.

questions_and_answers.question:
What is the main purpose of inheritance in
Java?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The main purpose is code reusability and establishing a
relationship between classes. It allows a new class to use the methods and
fields of an existing class, reducing redundancy and making the code easier to
maintain.

questions_and_answers.question: Explain why Java does not support multiple inheritance with classes.
questions_and_answers.answer:
Java avoids multiple inheritance to prevent the 'Diamond Problem'. If
a class could extend two parents that both have a method with the same
signature, the compiler wouldn't know which version to inherit, leading to
ambiguity and complexity.

questions_and_answers.question: When should you use the 'super' keyword in a constructor?
questions_and_answers.answer:
You must use 'super(...)' when the parent class does not have a
default (no-argument) constructor, or when you specifically want to pass
arguments to a parameterized constructor in the parent class to initialize its
fields.

No, this is called 'Method Hiding'.
Static methods belong to the class, not the instance. Therefore, they cannot be
overridden in the traditional OOP sense. The version of the method called
depends on the reference type, not the actual object type.

questions_and_answers.question:
What is the error in this
code: 'class A extends final B {}'?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The error is that class B is
marked as 'final'. In Java, a 'final' class cannot be extended by any other
class. This is a security and design feature to prevent modification of class
behavior.

questions_and_answers.question:
A
subclass method has 'private' access but overrides a 'public' method in the
parent. Is this allowed?
questions_and_answers.answer:
No. When overriding, the subclass method
cannot reduce the visibility of the parent method. Since 'private' is more
restrictive than 'public', the compiler will throw an error. You can, however,
make it more visible (e.g., overriding a protected method as public).

questions_and_answers.question:
Write a
snippet showing how to use the 'instanceof' operator safely.

How do you
correctly override the toString() method for a class 'Book' with fields 'title'
and 'author'?

practice_activities.task:
Create a class
'Shape' with a method area(). Create a subclass 'Circle' that overrides area()
to calculate the area of a circle using Math.PI.

Given a parent 'Vehicle' with a constructor taking 'brand',
write a 'Car' subclass constructor that correctly passes 'brand' to the parent.

Fix a code snippet where a subclass
tries to override a final method in the parent class.

Refactor two separate classes, 'Smartphone' and 'Laptop', into a
hierarchy using a common 'ElectronicDevice' superclass.

Add a toString() and equals() method to an existing
'User' class to allow proper comparison and printing.

Determine what happens when a parent reference
points to a child object and an overridden method is called.

Implement a 'Logger' system where 'FileLogger'
and 'ConsoleLogger' inherit from a base 'Logger' class.

options.D: It can call the parent's version of an overridden method.
